title: The Argument for Darkness
summary: A B2 paragraph passage arguing that better street lighting often means less light, not more, and that brightness has been mistaken for safety.
body:
When the small city of Flagstaff, in the high desert of Arizona, began restricting its outdoor lighting in the 1950s, the decision looked eccentric. Other towns were racing to flood their streets with light; Flagstaff was asking shopkeepers to point theirs downward. What few residents understood at the time was that the policy would later be celebrated as a model of efficiency. The reasoning is deceptively simple. A street lamp exists to illuminate a pavement, yet a great deal of the light produced by a conventional fixture never reaches the ground at all. It escapes sideways into the eyes of drivers, or straight up into a sky that has no use for it. That stray light is not merely wasted; it is paid for. Every watt that disappears into the clouds is a watt burned for nothing.

It is tempting to assume that the brightest street is the safest one, but the assumption rarely survives scrutiny. The human eye does not measure brightness in the way a meter does. What it responds to is contrast, and a single dazzling lamp set against deep shadow can leave a pedestrian harder to see, not easier. Glare of this kind floods the retina and forces the pupil to contract, so that the darker patches just beyond the lamp become, in effect, invisible. A row of dimmer, carefully shielded lights produces an even wash in which a cyclist or a child stands out clearly. The paradox is that turning the lights down can make the road more legible, provided the remaining light is aimed where it is needed.

What the shielded fixture demonstrates is a principle that runs through much of unglamorous engineering: efficiency is less about producing more and more about wasting less. The cheapest watt, as engineers like to say, is the one you never had to generate. By capping each lamp and tilting it toward the pavement, Flagstaff cut its energy bill while improving the view of the night sky, and in doing so it attracted the astronomers and the tourists who now sustain part of its economy. Chile made a similar calculation on a grander scale, regulating the lights of entire towns to protect the observatories perched in the Atacama. In neither case was darkness the goal. The goal was light that did its job and then stopped.

There is a quieter lesson buried in all this, and it has little to do with stars. We have grown so used to equating more energy with more progress that the idea of solving a problem by subtraction feels almost suspect. Yet the shielded lamp is not a sacrifice; it is a better tool, one that happens to use less. The objection that brighter is always safer turns out to be a habit of thought rather than a finding of physics. Had the engineers of the last century been asked to design a lamp from scratch, knowing what is known now about glare and the eye, it is hard to believe they would have built one that throws half its output at the sky. The technology that points light at the ground is neither new nor complicated. What was difficult was admitting that the obvious answer, the brighter answer, had been wrong all along.

annotations.meaningVi: lập dị, kỳ quặc
tràn ngập đường phố bằng ánh sáng
đơn giản một cách đánh lừa (tưởng đơn giản nhưng không hẳn)
chiếu sáng vỉa hè
thiết bị đèn thông thường
thoát ra theo phương ngang
ánh sáng lạc, ánh sáng đi sai hướng
đốt cháy (tiêu hao) một cách vô ích
hiếm khi đứng vững trước sự xem xét kỹ lưỡng
phản ứng với sự tương phản
ngọn đèn chói lóa
ánh sáng chói gắt
làm tràn ngập võng mạc
khiến đồng tử co lại
đèn được che chắn cẩn thận
một lớp ánh sáng đều đặn
dễ nhìn rõ hơn, dễ đọc hơn
lãng phí ít hơn
watt rẻ nhất
chụp/che phía trên mỗi ngọn đèn
nghiêng nó về phía vỉa hè
các đài thiên văn
nằm vắt vẻo trên sa mạc Atacama
giải quyết vấn đề bằng cách bớt đi
gần như đáng ngờ
một thói quen tư duy

annotations.phrase: throws half its output at the sky
annotations.meaningVi: hắt một nửa lượng ánh sáng lên trời
đã sai ngay từ đầu

grammarNotes.title: Cleft sentences with 'What'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu chẻ bắt đầu bằng 'What...' dùng để nhấn mạnh một thông tin: 'What few residents understood... was that...' (Điều mà ít cư dân hiểu được... là...). Cấu trúc: What + mệnh đề + be + phần nhấn mạnh.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Cleft sentences beginning with 'What' put the spotlight on a key idea: 'What the shielded fixture demonstrates is a principle...'. The pattern is What + clause + be + emphasised element.

grammarNotes.title: Concession with 'It is tempting to assume... but'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Cách nêu một giả định phổ biến rồi bác bỏ nó: 'It is tempting to assume that the brightest street is the safest one, but the assumption rarely survives scrutiny.' Đây là kiểu nhượng bộ tinh vi trong văn nghị luận B2.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A sophisticated concession move: acknowledge a common belief ('It is tempting to assume...'), then overturn it ('but the assumption rarely survives scrutiny'). Useful for building an argument.

grammarNotes.title: Past unreal conditional (third conditional)
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu điều kiện loại 3 nói về điều trái với quá khứ: 'Had the engineers... been asked to design a lamp from scratch... it is hard to believe they would have built...'. Lưu ý dạng đảo ngữ 'Had + S + p.p.' thay cho 'If + had'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Third conditional with inversion: 'Had the engineers been asked... they would have built...'. The inverted 'Had + subject + past participle' replaces 'If they had been asked'.

grammarNotes.title: Nominalisation in abstract argument
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Văn nghị luận trình độ cao thường biến động từ/tính từ thành danh từ: 'the assumption', 'the reasoning', 'the objection', 'efficiency'. Cách này giúp câu cô đọng và mang tính khái quát.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Higher-level argument relies on nominalisation, turning verbs and adjectives into nouns: 'the assumption', 'the reasoning', 'the objection'. This makes the prose dense and abstract.

translationVi:
Khi thành phố nhỏ Flagstaff, nằm trên sa mạc cao của bang Arizona, bắt đầu hạn chế ánh sáng ngoài trời vào những năm 1950, quyết định ấy trông có vẻ lập dị. Các thị trấn khác đang đua nhau tràn ngập đường phố bằng ánh sáng; còn Flagstaff lại yêu cầu các chủ cửa hàng chiếu đèn xuống dưới. Điều mà ít cư dân hiểu được vào thời điểm đó là chính sách này về sau sẽ được ca ngợi như một hình mẫu về hiệu quả. Lý lẽ thì đơn giản một cách đánh lừa. Một ngọn đèn đường tồn tại để chiếu sáng vỉa hè, vậy mà phần lớn ánh sáng do một thiết bị đèn thông thường tạo ra lại chẳng bao giờ chạm tới mặt đất. Nó thoát ra theo phương ngang vào mắt các tài xế, hoặc bay thẳng lên một bầu trời chẳng cần tới nó. Ánh sáng lạc ấy không chỉ bị lãng phí; nó còn phải trả tiền. Mỗi watt biến mất vào những đám mây là một watt bị đốt cháy một cách vô ích.

Thật dễ giả định rằng con phố sáng nhất là con phố an toàn nhất, nhưng giả định ấy hiếm khi đứng vững trước sự xem xét kỹ lưỡng. Mắt người không đo độ sáng theo cách một chiếc máy đo làm. Cái mà nó phản ứng là sự tương phản, và một ngọn đèn chói lóa đơn lẻ đặt giữa bóng tối sâu thẳm có thể khiến người đi bộ khó nhìn thấy hơn chứ không phải dễ hơn. Loại ánh sáng chói gắt này làm tràn ngập võng mạc và buộc đồng tử co lại, đến mức những khoảng tối ngay sau ngọn đèn trở nên, trên thực tế, vô hình. Một dãy đèn mờ hơn, được che chắn cẩn thận, tạo ra một lớp ánh sáng đều đặn trong đó một người đi xe đạp hay một đứa trẻ nổi bật rõ ràng. Nghịch lý là việc giảm đèn xuống lại có thể khiến mặt đường dễ nhìn rõ hơn, miễn là phần ánh sáng còn lại được hướng tới nơi cần thiết.

Điều mà thiết bị đèn có che chắn chứng minh là một nguyên tắc xuyên suốt phần lớn ngành kỹ thuật không hào nhoáng: hiệu quả ít liên quan đến việc tạo ra nhiều hơn mà liên quan nhiều hơn đến việc lãng phí ít đi. Watt rẻ nhất, như các kỹ sư hay nói, là watt mà bạn không bao giờ phải tạo ra. Bằng cách chụp che phía trên mỗi ngọn đèn và nghiêng nó về phía vỉa hè, Flagstaff đã cắt giảm hóa đơn năng lượng đồng thời cải thiện tầm nhìn lên bầu trời đêm, và nhờ vậy nó thu hút các nhà thiên văn và du khách, những người nay đang nuôi sống một phần nền kinh tế của thành phố. Chile cũng tính toán tương tự nhưng ở quy mô lớn hơn, điều chỉnh ánh sáng của cả những thị trấn để bảo vệ các đài thiên văn nằm vắt vẻo trên sa mạc Atacama. Trong cả hai trường hợp, bóng tối không phải là mục tiêu. Mục tiêu là thứ ánh sáng làm xong việc của nó rồi dừng lại.

Có một bài học lặng lẽ hơn ẩn trong tất cả những điều này, và nó chẳng liên quan mấy đến các vì sao. Chúng ta đã quá quen với việc đánh đồng nhiều năng lượng hơn với nhiều tiến bộ hơn, đến mức ý tưởng giải quyết một vấn đề bằng cách bớt đi gần như đáng ngờ. Vậy mà ngọn đèn có che chắn không phải là một sự hy sinh; nó là một công cụ tốt hơn, một công cụ tình cờ dùng ít năng lượng hơn. Phản bác rằng sáng hơn thì luôn an toàn hơn hóa ra là một thói quen tư duy chứ không phải một phát hiện của vật lý. Giả sử các kỹ sư của thế kỷ trước được yêu cầu thiết kế một ngọn đèn từ đầu, với những gì người ta biết hiện nay về ánh chói và con mắt, thật khó tin rằng họ sẽ chế tạo một ngọn đèn hắt một nửa lượng ánh sáng lên trời. Công nghệ hướng ánh sáng xuống đất không hề mới mẻ hay phức tạp. Cái khó là phải thừa nhận rằng câu trả lời hiển nhiên, câu trả lời sáng hơn, đã sai ngay từ đầu.

questions.prompt: Why did Flagstaff's lighting policy look 'eccentric' at first?
options: Other towns were adding light while Flagstaff aimed its light downward
It only allowed lighting near the observatory
It forced shops to close at night
questions.explanation: The text says 'Other towns were racing to flood their streets with light; Flagstaff was asking shopkeepers to point theirs downward.'
questions.hint: Look at the contrast drawn in the first paragraph between Flagstaff and other towns.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what happens to much of the light from a conventional street lamp?
options: It is reflected back by the pavement
It is stored for later use
It escapes sideways or upward and never reaches the ground
It is absorbed by the lamp itself
questions.explanation: The passage states the light 'escapes sideways into the eyes of drivers, or straight up into a sky that has no use for it.'
questions.hint: Focus on the description of where stray light goes in the first paragraph.

questions.prompt: What does the writer mean by 'a watt burned for nothing'?
options: Energy paid for but wasted because the light does no useful work
questions.explanation: The text says stray light 'is not merely wasted; it is paid for' and calls each lost watt one 'burned for nothing.'
questions.hint: Re-read the sentences about light being paid for at the end of paragraph one.

questions.prompt: What does the human eye primarily respond to, according to the passage?
questions.explanation: The text states 'What it responds to is contrast.'
questions.hint: See the second paragraph, where the eye is compared to a meter.

questions.prompt: Why can a single dazzling lamp make a pedestrian HARDER to see?
options: It attracts insects that block the view
Its glare floods the retina and makes darker areas effectively invisible
It flickers too quickly for the eye
questions.explanation: The passage explains that 'Glare of this kind floods the retina and forces the pupil to contract, so that the darker patches just beyond the lamp become, in effect, invisible.'
questions.hint: Look for the explanation of glare and the pupil in paragraph two.

questions.prompt: What is the 'paradox' the writer describes in the second paragraph?
options: Turning the lights down can make the road more legible
questions.explanation: The text says 'The paradox is that turning the lights down can make the road more legible.'
questions.hint: The paradox is stated directly near the end of paragraph two.

questions.prompt: According to the writer, what is efficiency mainly about?
questions.explanation: The passage states efficiency 'is less about producing more and more about wasting less.'
questions.hint: Check the opening of the third paragraph about unglamorous engineering.

questions.prompt: What is meant by 'the cheapest watt is the one you never had to generate'?
options: The best way to save energy is to avoid using it in the first place
questions.explanation: The saying frames avoided energy use as the cheapest, supporting the point that efficiency means 'wasting less.'
questions.hint: Connect this saying to the surrounding sentence about wasting less.

questions.prompt: What economic benefit did Flagstaff gain from its lighting policy?
options: It exported lamps to other towns
It attracted astronomers and tourists who now sustain part of its economy
questions.explanation: The text says it 'attracted the astronomers and the tourists who now sustain part of its economy.'
questions.hint: Look at the third paragraph after the lamp-capping description.

questions.prompt: Why did Chile regulate the lights of entire towns?
options: To save money on imported lamps
To protect the observatories in the Atacama
questions.explanation: The passage states Chile regulated lights 'to protect the observatories perched in the Atacama.'
questions.hint: See the comparison with Chile in the third paragraph.

questions.prompt: Which statement does the writer NOT claim?
options: Shielded lamps can use less energy
Darkness itself is the goal of these policies
Contrast matters more than raw brightness
questions.explanation: The writer explicitly rejects this, stating 'In neither case was darkness the goal.'
questions.hint: Find where the writer states what the goal was, and was not, in paragraph three.

questions.prompt: What does the writer say feels 'almost suspect' to modern thinking?
questions.explanation: The text says 'the idea of solving a problem by subtraction feels almost suspect.'
questions.hint: Look at the final paragraph's point about equating energy with progress.

questions.prompt: How does the writer describe the shielded lamp in the final paragraph?
options: A better tool that happens to use less
questions.explanation: The passage says 'the shielded lamp is not a sacrifice; it is a better tool, one that happens to use less.'
questions.hint: See how the writer corrects the idea of sacrifice near the end.

questions.prompt: What does the writer suggest about the belief that 'brighter is always safer'?
options: It is a habit of thought rather than a finding of physics
It is true only in deserts
questions.explanation: The text states the objection 'turns out to be a habit of thought rather than a finding of physics.'
questions.hint: Look for the sentence contrasting habit and physics in the last paragraph.

questions.prompt: What was the truly 'difficult' part of adopting better lighting, according to the conclusion?
options: Admitting that the brighter answer had been wrong
questions.explanation: The final sentence says 'What was difficult was admitting that the obvious answer, the brighter answer, had been wrong all along.'
questions.hint: The closing sentence states what was difficult, and it was not technical.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's overall attitude toward 'progress' in this passage?
options: Progress can sometimes mean using less, not more
Progress is impossible without new inventions
Progress should be measured in brightness
questions.explanation: The passage challenges the habit of 'equating more energy with more progress' and presents subtraction as a better solution.
questions.hint: Consider the broader argument running through the final paragraph.

cloze.template:
When the small city of Flagstaff, in the high desert of Arizona, began restricting its outdoor lighting in the 1950s, the decision looked {{b1}}. Other towns were racing to flood their streets with light; Flagstaff was asking shopkeepers to point theirs downward. What few residents understood at the time was that the policy would later be celebrated as a model of {{b2}}. The reasoning is deceptively {{b3}}. A street lamp exists to illuminate a pavement, yet a great deal of the light produced by a conventional fixture never reaches the {{b4}} at all. It escapes sideways into the eyes of drivers, or straight up into a sky that has no use for it. That stray light is not merely wasted; it is {{b5}} for. Every watt that disappears into the clouds is a watt burned for nothing.

It is tempting to assume that the {{b6}} street is the safest one, but the assumption rarely survives scrutiny. The human eye does not measure brightness in the way a meter does. What it responds to is {{b7}}, and a single dazzling lamp set against deep shadow can leave a pedestrian harder to see, not easier. Glare of this kind floods the {{b8}} and forces the pupil to contract, so that the darker patches just beyond the lamp become, in effect, invisible. A row of dimmer, carefully {{b9}} lights produces an even wash in which a cyclist or a child stands out clearly. The paradox is that turning the lights down can make the road more {{b10}}, provided the remaining light is aimed where it is needed.

What the shielded fixture demonstrates is a principle that runs through much of unglamorous engineering: efficiency is less about producing more and more about {{b11}} less. The cheapest watt, as engineers like to say, is the one you never had to generate. By capping each lamp and {{b12}} it toward the pavement, Flagstaff cut its energy bill while improving the view of the night sky, and in doing so it attracted the astronomers and the tourists who now sustain part of its economy. Chile made a similar calculation on a grander scale, regulating the lights of entire towns to protect the {{b13}} perched in the Atacama. In neither case was darkness the goal. The goal was light that did its job and then stopped.

There is a quieter lesson buried in all this, and it has little to do with stars. We have grown so used to equating more energy with more {{b14}} that the idea of solving a problem by {{b15}} feels almost suspect. Yet the shielded lamp is not a sacrifice; it is a better {{b16}}, one that happens to use less. The objection that brighter is always safer turns out to be a {{b17}} of thought rather than a finding of physics. Had the engineers of the last century been asked to design a lamp from scratch, knowing what is known now about glare and the eye, it is hard to believe they would have built one that throws half its output at the sky. The technology that points light at the ground is neither new nor complicated. What was difficult was admitting that the obvious answer, the brighter answer, had been {{b18}} all along.

blanks.explanation: The text says the decision 'looked eccentric' because it ran against the trend.
The policy was 'celebrated as a model of efficiency.'
Much of the light 'never reaches the ground at all.'
Stray light 'is not merely wasted; it is paid for.'
The tempting assumption is 'that the brightest street is the safest one.'
The text describes 'dimmer, carefully shielded lights.'
Turning lights down can make the road 'more legible.'
Flagstaff capped each lamp and was 'tilting it toward the pavement.'
Chile protected 'the observatories perched in the Atacama.'
We equate 'more energy with more progress.'
The idea of 'solving a problem by subtraction' feels suspect.
The shielded lamp is 'a better tool.'
The objection is 'a habit of thought rather than a finding of physics.'
The difficult part was admitting the brighter answer 'had been wrong all along.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer argues that 'brighter is safer' is a habit of thought rather than a finding of physics. Can you think of another everyday belief about technology or comfort where doing more, or using more, may actually solve the problem less well? Explain your reasoning.
